title: How to Start a Personal Curriculum Without Overplanning
strapline: A first day that feels calm enough to start, not heavy enough to avoid
signal: Messy planning pile transformed into a clear next step
sourceSubtitle: Good intentions, too many tabs, too many options, not enough clear next steps.

sourceItems.label: Five bookmarked curriculum links
sourceItems.detail: Each one looks promising, but none tells the learner what to do today

sourceItems.label: A big planning notebook
sourceItems.detail: Three pages of goals, categories, and future ideas

sourceItems.label: One strong article to read
sourceItems.detail: The first piece that could actually anchor today's work

sourceItems.label: A short related video
sourceItems.detail: Useful support, but only after the first task is clear

sourceItems.label: A simple make-something prompt
sourceItems.detail: A small output that proves the day is moving

tiktok.hook: Take this overplanned personal curriculum and turn it into one clear day.
tiktok.caption: The problem is not lack of good ideas. The problem is starting with too many of them at once.
beats: Open on the tabs, notes, and planning clutter.
Say that this is where a lot of self-directed learning dies before it starts.
Cut to the Today page with one reading task, one small output, and one reflection step.
End on the line about turning overplanning into a clear next step.
tiktok.cta: Lesson Hollow helps you start with a day, not drown in a plan.

tasks.title: Read one article that actually matters
tasks.note: Start with the piece that best matches today's interest

tasks.title: Watch one short supporting clip
tasks.note: Only one clip, only after the reading is grounded

tasks.title: Make a one-page idea map
tasks.note: Pull out the three ideas worth keeping from today's material

tasks.title: Pick tomorrow's next thread
tasks.note: Choose one direction instead of planning six
